titleStmt.title: A LETTER ON READING AND PATIENCE (synthetic sample)
titleStmt.author: Sample correspondent

bibl.title: A LETTER ON READING AND PATIENCE (synthetic sample)
bibl.author: Sample correspondent

revisionDesc.change: Synthetic structural twin of the line-level ZBZ Hersch encoding. The markup mirrors the real object (page breaks, line breaks, facsimile zones, standOff entities, @facs linking); every word of the text is invented placeholder prose and stands in for the rights-restricted original, which is local-only.

head.title: A LETTER ON READING AND PATIENCE

div.p: A book asks for the one thing we are least willing to give it,

which is time without a purpose. We open it expecting an answer
and grow impatient when it offers only a slower question. Yet
the page does not withhold its meaning out of malice; it waits
until the reader has stopped hurrying. To read well is to
consent to that delay, to let a sentence stand unfinished in
the mind until its weight settles. Marguerite Vautier once
wrote from Geneva that she had learned more from the
books she reread than from those she merely fin
ished.

The habit she described is not erudition but patience:
a willingness to be changed at the speed of the text.

There is a second patience that the first one teaches, harder
than the patience of waiting.

It is the patience of being misunderstood, of setting a thought
on the page and trusting that the attentive reader, whom one
will never meet, will return to it with more care than the first
glance allows. The writer who demands to be understood at once
writes for nobody, because no one reads at once. So I have
stopped polishing these lines for clarity and begun leaving in
them the small resistances that make a reader slow down.

If this letter has asked for your time without returning an
answer, that was its only honest subject.
